title: Congee with Century Egg

seo.title: Congee with Century Egg Recipe: Authentic Chinese Breakfast

Ingredients for Congee with Century Egg
Step-by-Step Instructions for Congee with Century Egg
seo.imageAlt: A bowl of creamy Congee with Century Egg garnished with green onions and a slice of century egg.
seo.imageCaption: Enjoy a comforting bowl of homemade Congee with Century Egg, a traditional Chinese breakfast.

description: Congee with Century Egg is a quintessential Chinese comfort food, a warm and savory rice porridge perfect for breakfast or any time you need a soothing meal. Its unique flavor comes from the distinct century egg, complemented by tender rice and often shredded pork and ginger. This dish offers a delightful blend of textures and a deeply satisfying taste that embodies traditional Chinese home cooking.

instructions.title: Prepare the Rice
instructions.text: Rinse 1 cup of jasmine rice thoroughly until the water runs clear. For a creamier congee, you can soak the rice in water for 30 minutes to an hour before cooking.

instructions.title: Start the Congee Base
instructions.text: In a large pot, combine the rinsed rice with 8-10 cups of water or chicken broth. Bring to a boil over high heat, then immediately reduce heat to low, cover, and simmer.

instructions.title: Prepare Add-ins
instructions.text: While the congee simmers, peel and dice the century eggs. If using pork, shred it finely. Julienne the ginger and chop the green onions, reserving some for garnish.

instructions.title: Cook the Congee
instructions.text: Continue to simmer the congee for at least 30-45 minutes, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking, until the rice grains have broken down and the porridge has thickened to your desired consistency. Add more hot water if it becomes too thick.

instructions.title: Add Century Egg and Pork
instructions.text: Once the congee reaches the desired consistency, add the diced century eggs, shredded pork (if using), and most of the julienned ginger. Stir well and cook for another 5-10 minutes, allowing the flavors to meld.

instructions.title: Season and Serve
instructions.text: Season the congee with salt and white pepper to taste. Ladle the hot congee into bowls. Drizzle with a little sesame oil, garnish with the remaining green onions and julienned ginger, and serve immediately.

tips: For an extra creamy congee, freeze the rinsed rice for a few hours or overnight before cooking. The freezing process breaks down the rice grains, making them softer and quicker to cook into a porridge.
Don't be afraid to adjust the consistency of the congee. If it's too thick, add more hot water or broth. If it's too thin, simmer uncovered for a bit longer.
Congee is highly customizable. Feel free to add other ingredients like salted duck egg, peanuts, or crispy fried dough (youtiao) for extra texture and flavor.
article:
Embrace the comforting embrace of a warm bowl of congee, a staple in Chinese cuisine that offers solace and sustenance in equal measure. While plain congee is a beloved canvas, one particular variation stands out for its unique flavor profile and intriguing ingredients: Congee with Century Egg. This classic Chinese breakfast dish is a testament to the versatility of rice porridge, transforming a simple base into a deeply savory and wonderfully textured meal. If you're looking to explore the authentic flavors of a Chinese morning, this recipe is an excellent starting point.

## What is Congee with Century Egg?

At its heart, congee is a rice porridge, typically made by simmering rice in a large amount of water or broth until the grains break down into a thick, creamy consistency. It’s a versatile dish, often served plain with various toppings, or cooked with ingredients directly mixed in. Congee with Century Egg, known in Mandarin as "Pidan Shou Rou Zhou" (literally "century egg lean meat porridge"), takes this humble base and elevates it with two key components: lean pork and, of course, the distinctive century egg.

The century egg, or "pidan," is a preserved duck, chicken, or quail egg that has undergone a fascinating curing process, typically for several weeks or months, in a mixture of clay, ash, salt, quicklime, and rice hulls. This process transforms the egg's appearance and flavor dramatically. The albumen (egg white) turns into a translucent, dark brown jelly-like substance, while the yolk becomes a creamy, dark green or grey, often with an ammonia-like aroma that mellows into a rich, complex umami flavor once cooked or paired correctly. When diced and stirred into the hot, creamy congee, the century egg imparts a unique savoriness and a slightly pungent, earthy depth that is surprisingly addictive. The lean pork adds another layer of flavor and protein, making the dish incredibly satisfying and hearty.

## Ingredients for Congee with Century Egg

Crafting a delicious bowl of Congee with Century Egg doesn't require an extensive list of ingredients, but the quality of each component does make a difference.

For the Congee Base:
*   1 cup white rice (short or medium grain preferred for creaminess)
*   8-10 cups water or chicken/pork broth (broth significantly enhances flavor)
*   1-2 inches fresh ginger, sliced or julienned

For the Main Additions:
*   2-3 century eggs, peeled and diced
*   6-8 oz lean pork (e.g., pork loin, tenderloin), thinly sliced or minced
*   1 tsp soy sauce (for marinating pork)
*   1 tsp cornstarch (for marinating pork)
*   1/2 tsp sesame oil (for marinating pork)

For Seasoning and Garnish:
*   Salt to taste
*   White pepper to taste
*   2-3 scallions (green onions), chopped
*   A drizzle of sesame oil (for serving)
*   Optional: A dash of soy sauce, fried shallots, or chili oil for serving

## Step-by-Step Instructions for Congee with Century Egg

Making Congee with Century Egg is a patient process, but the results are well worth the wait.

1.  **Prepare the Rice:** Rinse the rice thoroughly under cold water until the water runs clear. This removes excess starch and prevents the congee from becoming too gummy. For a faster cooking time, you can soak the rice in water for 30 minutes, or even freeze it overnight (the freezing and thawing process helps break down the rice grains).
2.  **Marinate the Pork:** If using lean pork, slice it very thinly or mince it. In a small bowl, combine the pork with 1 tsp soy sauce, 1 tsp cornstarch, and 1/2 tsp sesame oil. Mix well and set aside to marinate for at least 15 minutes. This step helps tenderize the pork and infuse it with flavor.
3.  **Prepare the Century Eggs:** Carefully peel the century eggs. They can be a bit sticky, so wetting your knife can help. Dice the century eggs into small, even pieces. Set aside.
4.  **Cook the Congee Base:** In a large, heavy-bottomed pot, combine the rinsed rice with 8-10 cups of water or broth. Bring the mixture to a rolling boil over high heat, stirring occasionally to prevent the rice from sticking to the bottom. Once boiling, reduce the heat to the lowest setting, cover the pot partially (leaving a small gap for steam to escape), and simmer.
5.  **Simmer and Stir:** Continue to simmer the congee for 1 to 1.5 hours, stirring every 15-20 minutes, especially during the last 30 minutes, to prevent sticking and ensure even cooking. The goal is for the rice grains to break down and release their starch, creating a thick, creamy porridge. If the congee becomes too thick, you can add more hot water or broth.
6.  **Add Main Ingredients:** Once the congee has reached your desired creamy consistency, add the sliced or julienned ginger to the pot. Stir in the marinated pork, breaking it up as it cooks. The pork will cook quickly, usually within 5-7 minutes. Then, add most of the diced century eggs, reserving a small amount for garnish. Stir everything together gently.
7.  **Season and Finish:** Season the Congee with Century Egg with salt and white pepper to taste. Give it a final gentle stir.
8.  **Serve:** Ladle the hot congee into individual bowls. Garnish with the reserved diced century egg pieces, freshly chopped scallions, and a drizzle of sesame oil.

## Tips for Perfect Congee

Achieving that perfect bowl of creamy, comforting congee is an art, but these tips will guide you:

*   **Rice Choice:** Short or medium-grain white rice (like Jasmine or Calrose) yields the creamiest congee. Long-grain rice can work but results in a less velvety texture.
*   **Liquid Ratio:** The 1 cup rice to 8-10 cups liquid ratio is a good starting point. Adjust based on your preferred thickness. More liquid for thinner congee, less for thicker.
*   **Low and Slow:** Patience is key. A slow simmer allows the rice grains to break down properly, creating that signature creamy texture. High heat will cook the rice but won't achieve the same consistency.
*   **Stirring:** Stirring prevents sticking and also helps the rice grains release their starch, contributing to the creaminess. Be diligent, especially in the last stages of cooking.
*   **Broth is Best:** While water works, using chicken or pork broth adds incredible depth of flavor that plain water simply can't match.
*   **Don't Skimp on Ginger:** Ginger is essential for balancing the richness and unique flavor of the century egg.
*   **Adjusting Consistency:** If your congee gets too thick while cooking or after resting, simply stir in a bit more hot water or broth until it reaches your desired consistency.

## Serving Suggestions & Pairings

Congee with Century Egg is a meal in itself, but it truly shines when paired with traditional accompaniments. Serve it piping hot for the best experience.

*   **Garnishes:** Beyond the essential scallions, reserved century egg pieces, and sesame oil, consider a dash of white pepper, a few drops of soy sauce, or a sprinkle of crispy fried shallots or peanuts for added texture. A drizzle of chili oil is fantastic for those who enjoy a bit of heat.
*   **Youtiao (Chinese Fried Dough Sticks):** This is perhaps the most classic pairing. Dip crispy youtiao into the warm congee, allowing them to soak up the savory flavors.
*   **Pickled Vegetables:** A small side of pickled radish, preserved tofu (fermented bean curd), or pickled mustard greens provides a refreshing, tangy contrast to the rich congee.
*   **Other Dim Sum:** Congee is often served as part of a larger dim sum breakfast spread, alongside steamed buns, dumplings, or spring rolls.
*   **Hot Tea:** A pot of hot Chinese tea, such as Jasmine or Pu-erh, is the perfect beverage to cleanse the palate and complement the meal.

## Variations & Substitutions

While Congee with Century Egg is a classic, you can easily adapt this recipe to suit your preferences or what you have on hand.

*   **Different Proteins:** Instead of lean pork, try shredded chicken, thinly sliced fish (often added towards the end of cooking to prevent overcooking), ground beef, or even seafood like shrimp or dried scallops for an intensified umami flavor.
*   **Vegetarian Option:** Omit the pork and ensure you use vegetable broth instead of chicken or pork broth. The century egg itself is not vegetarian (as it's an egg), but it can be enjoyed by ovo-vegetarians. For a fully vegan version, you'd omit the century egg and focus on mushrooms, tofu, and other vegetables.
*   **Added Vegetables:** Stir in leafy greens like spinach, bok choy, or Chinese broccoli during the last 10 minutes of cooking for added nutrients and color. Sliced mushrooms (shiitake or oyster) also work wonderfully.
*   **Flavor Boosters:** A small amount of dried scallops, rehydrated and shredded, can be added to the congee base for an incredible depth of seafood umami. A touch of five-spice powder can also enhance the pork flavor.
*   **Spice Level:** For a kick, add a few slices of fresh red chili or a generous swirl of chili oil directly into your bowl.
*   **Century Egg Alternatives:** While integral to this dish, if century eggs are unavailable, preserved salted duck eggs offer a different but complementary savory and slightly briny flavor. Dice them and add them the same way.

Congee with Century Egg is more than just a breakfast; it's a bowl of tradition, comfort, and unique flavors. Its creamy texture, combined with the distinct savory notes of century egg and tender pork, creates a deeply satisfying experience. Don't be intimidated by the century egg; its intriguing taste is what makes this dish so special and beloved. So, gather your ingredients, take your time, and enjoy the process of creating this wholesome and delicious Chinese culinary masterpiece in your own kitchen.
